contraventionDescription: Parked in a disc parking place without clearly displaying a valid disc – Contravention Code 35

sections.heading: Parked in a disc parking place without clearly displaying a valid disc – Contravention Code 35
sections.content: You may also wish to check the information under contravention code 19 when you have finished with this page.
sections.contentHtml: You may also wish to check the information under contravention code 19 when you have finished with this page.

sections.heading: How to have your penalty charge notice (PCN) for this parking contravention cancelled.
sections.content: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.
sections.contentHtml: We may be able to assist you in having your penalty charge notice for this contravention cancelled.

sections.heading: Follow our 3 stage process:
sections.content:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.
2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.
3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.
sections.contentHtml:
1 Check: Specific grounds for appeal for this contravention (below on this page)
This may also include diagrams and pictures showing signage requirements etc.

2 Check: General grounds for appeal
Applies to most contraventions.

3 Check: Summarised parking adjudicators decisions
Includes details of example cases won.

sections.heading: Specific grounds of appeal for this contravention
sections.content:
1. We hear of penalty charge notices (parking tickets)being issued for this offence even when the pay and display tickets are clearly visible. It may be that you had displayed it in a side window, attached it to the front windscreen or simply placed it on the dash board. Sometimes the adhesive fails and it is possible for a ticket, especially in damp weather, to fall off of the windscreen and land upside down. Sometimes the window fogs up on the inside and the ticket is not visible. If you have a pay and display ticket you should send it in to a local authority confirming it either was displayed clearly or that due to the failure of the adhesive it had fallen off of the wind screen, etc.
Below we show diagrams of the signs and bay markings which must be in situ by law and much more information that will help you decide whether to appeal against your penalty charge notice.
2. In some areas (Westminster for example) you must purchase the ticket from the pay and display machine on the same side of the Road as where you are parked. However sometimes the Machine on the same side of the Road is out of order in which case you should purchase a ticket from the nearest alternative machine wherever it is or place a note in your windscreen. If you receive a penalty charge notice then simply write in and explain the situation and the PCN should be cancelled. Just watch out that the machine that you actually purchase from is in the same borough as where you actually park!
3. We have heard of instances of motorists who did not display their ticket, borrowing tickets from other motorists and sending it in with a representation as proof that they had in fact displayed the ticket, although of course we cannot condone such actions.
4. Was there clear road-side signage indicating parking restrictions on each row of parking bays and within a maximum of 15 metres (3 car lengths) of where you actually parked. The signs should be a maximum of 30 metres apart and within 5  metres of the bay end. Were the times of enforcement clearly shown on either the sign or on the pay and display machine.  London authorities such as Westminster (which issues more tickets than any other local authority) do not put the controlled hours on the signs.
5. Quite often motorists are confused by poor signage, for example where pay and display bays join meter bays. Camden council in London are particularly poor at signposting their various restrictions. If the signage is confusing you should take photographs and make representations. If the signage is not clear and does not comply then the contravention did not occur. If you have different types of bays adjoining each other for example pay and display bays adjoining residents parking bays then there should be a sign at the point where the bays meet (or within 5 metres of the end of the bay) with arrows pointing each way and clearly showing the different types of enforcement. We do not believe it sufficient for there to be just demarcation lines in the Road.
6. The Traffic Sign’s Regulations 1994 states that the designated width of a parking bay should be 180 cm and the maximum should be 270 cm. The length should be at least 4.5 metres.
 
Please see our section on parking adjudicators decisions which will have details of cases for this contravention.
 
Below: The required dimensions of an individually marked pay and display bay. Please note the permitted variants in the box below the diagram. Obviously if the bay isn’t to the correct dimensions then you may want to appeal. There are no guarantees but the bay should really be compliant in order for it to be enforceable.

Below: Diagram of the sign with the required dimensions.
Back to Parking
